description.en: Start your journey with the basics of Arabic grammar.
description.ar: ابدأ رحلتك مع أساسيات قواعد اللغة العربية.
description.fr: Commencez votre voyage avec les bases de la grammaire arabe.
description.de: Beginne deine Reise mit den Grundlagen der arabischen Grammatik.
description.ru: Начните свое путешествие с основ арабской грамматики.
description.zh: 从阿拉伯语语法基础开始您的旅程。

lessons.content: The Arabic alphabet consists of 28 letters. It is written from right to left. All letters are consonants, while vowels are indicated by marks above or below the letters.

Arabic letters change their shape depending on their position in the word: beginning, middle, end, or isolated. Most letters connect to both sides, but some 'stubborn' letters only connect to the right.
This group includes Alif (the first letter) and the 'Boat Family' (Ba, Ta, Tha) which share a similar base shape but differ in dot placement.

examples.english: Bayt (House) - Starts with Ba (1 dot below)
Tuffah (Apple) - Starts with Ta (2 dots above)
Tha'lab (Fox) - Starts with Tha (3 dots above)

lessons.content: Jim, Ha, and Kha share a similar semi-circle shape. Jim has a dot inside, Ha has no dots, and Kha has a dot above.
These letters are 'non-connectors' from the left side. Dal/Dhal are small curves; Ra/Zay dip below the line.
Sin/Shin have 'teeth'. Sad/Dad have a loop shape. Note the emphasis in Sad and Dad (heavy letters).
Tah and Zah are emphasized loops. 'Ayn and Ghayn have a small curve above and a larger one below. Ghayn has a dot.
Fa has one dot, Qaf has two dots. Kaf has a unique 'S' shape inside when at the end.
The final group. Lam looks like a hook. Mim is a small loop. Nun has a dot. Ha is a circle. Waw and Ya are also long vowels.
Short vowels are marks placed above or below letters to determine pronunciation. The three main short vowels are Fatha (a), Kasra (i), and Damma (u).
Long vowels are elongations of short vowels. Alif extends Fatha, Ya extends Kasra, and Waw extends Damma.
Sukoon indicates the absence of a vowel (stop). Shadda indicates a doubled consonant, emphasized by holding the sound slightly longer.
Subject pronouns are used to indicate the doer of the action or the subject of a nominal sentence. They are detached and independent.
Object pronouns are attached suffixes connected to verbs to indicate who receive the action.
Possession is expressed by attaching suffix pronouns to the end of nouns.
A nominal sentence (Jumla Ismiyya) starts with a noun or pronoun. It consists of a Subject (Mubtada) and Predicate (Khabar) and typically corresponds to 'is/are' sentences in English.
A verbal sentence (Jumla Fi'liyya) starts with a verb. The standard order is: Verb + Subject + Object.

examples.english: Kataba al-talibu al-darsa (The student wrote the lesson)
Ya'kulu al-rajulu al-tuffaha (The man eats the apple)

lessons.content: While Verb-Subject-Object (VSO) is standard for verbal sentences, Subject-Verb-Object (SVO) is also common in nominal sentences or for emphasis.

examples.english: Dhahaba Ahmed ila al-madrasa (Ahmed went to school - VSO)
Ahmed dhahaba ila al-madrasa (Ahmed went to school - SVO)

lessons.content: In verbal sentences starting with a verb, the verb is usually singular even if the subject is plural. However, in nominal sentences, the verb must match the subject in number.

examples.english: Dakhala al-tullabu (The students entered - Verb Singular)
Al-tullabu dakhalu (The students entered - Verb Plural)

lessons.content: All Arabic nouns are either masculine or feminine. Feminine nouns usually end with Taa Marbuta (ة).
Arabic has three numbers: Singular (one), Dual (two), and Plural (three or more). Dual ends in 'aan' (ان) or 'ayn' (ين).
Sound Masculine Plural adds 'oon' (ون) or 'een' (ين). Sound Feminine Plural adds 'aat' (ات).
Broken plurals do not follow a regular rule; the internal structure of the word changes. These must be memorized.
Indefinite nouns usually take Tanween (un/an/in). Definite nouns start with 'Al-' (ال) and lose the Tanween.
Arabic nouns change their ending vowel based on their role in the sentence: Marfu' (Nominative - u), Mansub (Accusative - a), Majrur (Genitive - i).
Adjectives follow the noun they describe. They must match in Gender, Number, Definiteness, and Case.
Common adjectives for describing people, objects, and places.
Demonstratives point to specific things. 'Hadha' (this) is for masculine near, 'Hadhihi' (this) for feminine near. 'Dhalika' (that) is for masculine far, 'Tilka' (that) for feminine far.
Adding 'Al-' makes a noun definite. Note the visible difference between Moon letters (pronounced clearly) and Sun letters (merged with the following letter).
A noun without 'Al-' and typically with Tanween is indefinite, equivalent to 'a' or 'an' in English.
Prepositions connect words. Common ones include: Min (from), Ila (to), Fi (in), 'Ala (on). They cause the following noun to take the Genitive case (Kasra).
A combination of a preposition/adverb and a noun forms a 'Shibh Jumla' (Quote-sentence) which often acts as the Predicate (Khabar) in a nominal sentence.

examples.english: Al-kitabu 'ala al-maktabi (The book is on the desk)

lessons.content: Arabic uses specific tools to form questions. Most are particles or nouns placed at the beginning of the sentence. Key tools include: Ma/Madha (What), Man (Who), Ayna (Where), Kayfa (How), Mata (When), Limadha (Why), Kam (How many), Ayy (Which).

examples.english: Ma hadha? (What is this? - for nouns)
Madha taf'alu? (What are you doing? - for verbs)
Kam as-sa'atu? (What time is it? / How much?)

lessons.content: To form Yes/No questions, place 'Hal' (هل) or 'A' (أ) at the beginning of the sentence.

examples.english: Hal anta talibun? (Are you a student?)
Na'am, ana talibun (Yes, I am a student)

lessons.content: Wahid (1), Ithnan (2), Thalatha (3), Arba'a (4), Khamsa (5), Sitta (6), Sab'a (7), Thamaniya (8), Tis'a (9), Ashara (10).
Awwal (1st), Thani (2nd), Thalith (3rd), Rabi' (4th), Khamis (5th).

description.en: Expand your knowledge with complex sentence structures and verb forms.
description.ar: وسع معرفتك بتراكيب الجمل المعقدة وصيغ الأفعال.
description.fr: Développez vos connaissances avec des structures de phrases complexes et des formes verbales.
description.de: Erweitere dein Wissen mit komplexen Satzstrukturen und Verbformen.
description.ru: Расширьте свои знания с помощью сложных структур предложений и глагольных форм.
description.zh: 通过复杂的句子结构和动词形式扩展您的知识。

lessons.content: The Past Tense (Al-Madi) indicates an action completed in the past. It is the base form of the verb (root). Example: 'Kataba' (He wrote).

examples.english: Kataba at-talibu ad-darsa (The student wrote the lesson)
Dhahabtu ila as-suqi (I went to the market)

lessons.content: The Present Tense (Al-Mudaric) indicates an ongoing or habitual action. It starts with a prefix (Alif, Nun, Ya, Ta). Example: 'Yaktubu' (He writes).

examples.english: Yaktubu at-talibu ad-darsa (The student writes the lesson)
Nadhhabu ila al-madrasati (We go to school)

lessons.content: The Imperative (Al-Amr) is used for commands. It is derived from the Present Jussive. Example: 'Uktub' (Write!).
To negate Past: 'Ma' + Past. To negate Present: 'La' + Present. Future negation: 'Lan' + Present (Accusative). Past negation (Formal): 'Lam' + Present (Jussive).
Weak verbs contain a vowel (Waw, Ya, Alif) in the root. They follow special conjugation rules. Example: 'Wajada' (He found - Assimilated), 'Qala' (He said - Hollow), 'Daa' (He called - Defective).
Hollow verbs have a vowel as the middle radical (e.g., Qala, Nama). The middle vowel often changes or disappears in conjugation.
Verbs with a Hamza in the root (e.g., Akala, Sa'ala, Qara'a). The Hamza is treated as a strong consonant but has spelling rules.
The base form of the verb. Most 3-letter verbs belong to this form. It expresses the basic meaning.
Doubling the middle letter. Often indicates causation or intensity. 'Alima (knew) -> 'Allama (taught).
Adding Alif after the first letter. Often expresses an action done with someone else (associative).
Prefixed with Alif. Isative (making someone do something). Jalasa (sat) -> Ajlasa (seated someone).
Reflexive of Form II. 'Allama (taught) -> Ta'allama (learned/taught himself).
Reflexive/Reciprocal of Form III. Ta'awana (cooperated with each other).
Prefixed with In-. Passive/Reflexive. Kasara (broke) -> Inkasara (got broken).
Inserted 'ta' after first radical. Reflexive/Middle voice. Jama'a (gathered) -> Ijtama'a (met/gathered together).
Rare. Mostly for colors and defects. Amar (red) -> Ihmara (turned red/blushed).
Prefixed with Ista-. Requesting or seeking something. Ghafara (forgave) -> Istaghfara (sought forgiveness).
Adverbs of time (Zaman) and place (Makan) specify when or where an action happens. They are usually Accusative (Mansub). Examples: Yawm (day), Layl (night), Fawqa (above), Tahta (below).

examples.english: Al-kitabu fawqa al-maktabi (The book is above the desk)

lessons.content: The receiver of the action. It is always Accusative (Mansub). Signs: Fatha (singular), Ya (dual/plural masculine), Kasra (plural feminine).

examples.english: Akala al-waladu at-tuffahata (The boy ate the apple)

lessons.content: A noun derived from the same root as the verb, used for emphasis or to describe the type/number of action.

examples.english: Darabtuhu darban (I hit him a hitting [severely])
Mashaytu mashya al-asadi (I walked the walk of a lion)

lessons.content: Explains the reason for the action. Usually an indefinite noun in Accusative case. Answers 'Why?'.

examples.english: Waqaftu ihtiraman lil-mu'allimi (I stood up out of respect for the teacher)

lessons.content: Describes the state of the subject or object when the action took place. Answers 'How?'. Usually indefinite and Accusative.

examples.english: Ja'a al-waladu bakiyan (The boy came crying)
Sharibtu al-ma'a baridan (I drank the water [while it was] cold)

lessons.content: Idafa consists of Mudaf (Possessed - No 'Al', No Tanween) + Mudaf Ilayhi (Possessor - Genitive case). Example: 'Kitab al-waladi' (The boy's book).

examples.english: Babu al-bayti (The door of the house)

lessons.content: Many modern terms are formed using Idafa. Example: 'Ghurfat an-nawm' (Room of sleep -> Bedroom).
Compound numbers. Read the ones digit first, then the tens. Example: 21 is 'Wahid wa 'ishrun' (One and twenty).
Mi'a (100), Alf (1000). They do not change gender.
Rules summary: 3-10 (Plural Genitive, Opposite Gender), 11-99 (Singular Accusative), 100+ (Singular Genitive).

examples.english: Thalathatu kutubin (3 books - Opposite gender, plural majrur)
Ahada 'ashara kawkaban (11 stars - Matching gender, singular mansub)

lessons.content: Negates Present tense (General negation). Also used for prohibition (La Taf'al - Don't do).
Negates Past meaning using Jussive Present form. 'Lam yadhhab' (He did not go). Formal and strong.
Negates Future tense. Followed by Accusative (Mansub) Present form. 'Lan adhhaba' (I will not go).
Negates Nominal Sentences. It acts like Kana (Sisters of Kana). 'Laysa al-jawwu baridan' (The weather is not cold).

examples.english: Laysa al-mudiru mawjudan (The manager is not present)

lessons.content: Negates Past tense verbs mostly. 'Ma akaltu' (I did not eat). Can also negate nominal sentences (Ma al-Hjaziyya).
The most common conjunction. Links words or sentences without implying order. 'Ahmad wa Zaid' (Ahmad and Zaid).

examples.english: Ja'a Ahmadu wa Muhammadun (Ahmad and Muhammad came)

lessons.content: Implies immediate sequence or cause. 'Dakhalat al-mu'allim fa-waqafa at-tullab' (The teacher entered, so the students stood up).

examples.english: Saqata al-kubu fa-inkasara (The cup fell and broke immediately)

lessons.content: Implies sequence with a time delay. 'Dhahabtu ila al-madrasa thumma raja'tu' (I went to school, then returned [after a while]).

examples.english: Zara'a al-fallahu al-qamha thumma hasadahu (The farmer planted wheat then harvested it)

lessons.content: Used for choice or doubt. 'Tuffah aw Burtuqal' (Apple or Orange).

examples.english: Ishrab ma'an aw 'asiran (Drink water or juice)

lessons.content: Used to correct a previous statement. 'Ma ja'a Zaidun bal 'Amrun' (Zaid didn't come, rather Amr [came]).

examples.english: La uridu shayan bal qahwatan (I don't want tea, but rather coffee)

lessons.content: Used for contrast. 'Al-baytu saghirun lakinnehu jamilun' (The house is small but beautiful).

examples.english: Ash-shamsu sati'atun lakinna al-jawwa baridun (The sun is bright but the weather is cold)

lessons.content: Used for singular masculine nouns (animate or inanimate). 'Al-waladu alladhi...' (The boy who...).

examples.english: Ja'a at-talibu alladhi najaha (The student who passed came)

lessons.content: Used for singular feminine nouns and plural irrational nouns. 'Al-bintu allati...' (The girl who...).

examples.english: Hadhihi al-bintu allati fazat (This is the girl who won)

lessons.content: Used for masculine plural persons. 'Ar-rijalu alladhina...' (The men who...).

examples.english: Uhibbu alladhina yasduquna (I like those who tell the truth)

lessons.content: General relative pronoun for rational beings (people). 'Ahsin ila man ahsana ilayka' (Do good to whoever did good to you).

examples.english: Man yajtahid yanjah (Whoever works hard succeeds)

lessons.content: General relative pronoun for irrational things. 'Af'alu ma uridu' (I do what I want).

examples.english: Sa-ashtari ma tuhibbu (I will buy what you like)

description.en: Master stylistic nuances and complex grammatical structures.
description.ar: أتقن الفروق الدقيقة والمكتبات النحوية المعقدة.
description.fr: Maîtrisez les nuances stylistiques et les structures grammaticales complexes.
description.de: Meistern Sie stilistische Nuancen und komplexe grammatikalische Strukturen.
description.ru: Освойте стилистические нюансы и сложные грамматические структуры.
description.zh: 掌握风格细微差别和复杂的语法结构。

lessons.content: Particles that enter a nominal sentence. They make the Subject Accusative (Mansub) and the Predicate Nominative (Marfu). Includes: Inna (Emphasis), Anna (That), Lakinna (But), Layta (Wish), La'alla (Hope).

examples.english: Inna al-'ilma nurun (Verily, knowledge is light)
Layta ash-shababa ya'udu (I wish youth would return)

lessons.content: Verbs that enter a nominal sentence. They make the Subject Nominative (Marfu) and the Predicate Accusative (Mansub). Includes: Kana (Was), Asbaha (Became), Laysa (Is not), Mazala (Still is).

examples.english: Kana al-jawwu baridan (The weather was cold)
Asbaha al-'alamu qaryatan saghiratan (The world became a small village)

lessons.content: Verbs of thought/certainty. They take two objects (both Accusative). Examples: Zanna (Thought), Ra'a (Saw/Considered), 'Alima (Knew).

examples.english: Zanantu al-imtihana sahlan (I thought the exam was easy)

lessons.content: Formed by changing vowels: Damma on first letter, Kasra before last. 'Kataba' -> 'Kutiba' (It was written).
Formed by changing vowels: Damma on prefix, Fatha before last. 'Yaktubu' -> 'Yuktabu' (It is written).
Used for possible conditions in the future. Takes Jussive (Majzum) verbs if they are Present tense. 'In tadhhab adhhab' (If you go, I go).

examples.english: In tadrus tanjah (If you study, you succeed)

lessons.content: Used for likely or certain conditions. Often followed by Past tense verbs with Future meaning.

examples.english: Idha ja'a nasru Allah (When the victory of Allah comes)

lessons.content: Used for impossible or hypothetical conditions (Counterfactual). 'Law' implies the abstention of the result due to the abstention of the condition.

examples.english: Law ijtahadta la-najahta (If you had worked hard, you would have succeeded [but you didn't])

lessons.content: Followed by a noun (Nominative). 'Lawla al-'ilmu la-halaka an-nasu' (If not for knowledge, people would have perished).

examples.english: Lawla al-ma'u la-mata az-zar'u (If not for water, the crops would have died)

lessons.content: Particles that affect two verbs (Condition and Result), making them Jussive. Includes: In, Man, Ma, Mahma, Ayna, Mata.

examples.english: Man ya'mal su'an yujza bihi (Whoever does evil will be recompensed for it)

lessons.content: A relative clause (Jumlat as-Sila) describes a definite noun. It must contain a pronoun (A'id) referring back to the noun.

examples.english: Ar-rajulu alladhi ra'aytuhu karimun (The man whom I saw is generous - 'hu' matches 'Ar-rajul')

lessons.content: Special uses of 'Ma' and 'Man'. Using 'Alladhi' as a connector in complex narration. Omitting the relative pronoun (in indefinite clauses).

examples.english: Ra'aytu taliban yaqra'u (I saw a student [who is] reading - No 'Alladhi' for indefinite)

lessons.content: Connectors that explain the reason. 'Li-anna' is followed by a nominal sentence. 'Bi-sabab' is followed by a noun.

examples.english: Ghaba li-annahu maridun (He was absent because he is sick)

lessons.content: Connectors showing purpose. Followed by Subjunctive (Mansub) Present tense. 'Adrusu li-anjaha' (I study to succeed).
Connectors showing the result or consequence. 'Fa' implies immediate result.

examples.english: Ijtahadtu fa-najahtu (I worked hard, so I succeeded)

lessons.content: Using particles like 'Inna', 'Lam al-Ibtida', 'Qad' (with Past tense) to emphasize meaning.

examples.english: Qad aflaha al-mu'minuna (Certainly, the believers have succeeded)
Wallahi innahu la-sadiqun (By Allah, he is indeed truthful)

lessons.content: Restricting the meaning using 'Innama' (Only/Just) or 'Ma... Illa' (Nothing... Except).

examples.english: Innama al-mu'minuna ikhwatun (The believers are but brothers)
Ma Muhammadun illa rasulun (Muhammad is but a messenger)

lessons.content: The main tool for exception. The noun after 'Illa' is usually Accusative (Mansub) if the sentence is affirmative. If negative, it can be Accusative or match the case of the person/thing excepted from (Badal).

examples.english: Hadara at-tullabu illa Sa'idan (The students attended except Sa'id)

lessons.content: These act as nouns. The word after them is always Genitive (Mudaf Ilayh). The tools 'Ghayr' and 'Siwa' themselves take the case that the word after 'Illa' would have taken.

examples.english: Hadara at-tullabu ghayra Sa'idin (The students attended except [other than] Sa'id)

lessons.content: A noun that follows another noun to clarify or represent it. Types include: Total Badal (The leader Ahmad), Partial Badal (I ate the apple, its half), or Quality Badal (I liked the student, his knowledge).

examples.english: Ja'a al-qa'idu Ahmadu (The leader, Ahmad, came - Total Badal)
Akaltu ar-raghifa nisfahu (I ate the loaf, half of it - Partial Badal)

lessons.content: An indefinite Accusative (Mansub) noun used to remove ambiguity from a preceding word (Number, Weight, Measure) or a sentence.

examples.english: Ishtaraytu 'ishrina kitaban (I bought twenty books - Specification for number)
Ana aktharu minka malan (I have more wealth than you - Specification for sentence)

description.en: Dive deep into classical Arabic, rhetoric, and advanced linguistic analysis.
description.ar: تعمق في اللغة العربية الفصحى والبلاغة والتحليل اللغوي المتقدم.
description.fr: Plongez dans l'arabe classique, la rhétorique et l'analyse linguistique avancée.
description.de: Tauchen Sie tief in das klassische Arabisch, Rhetorik und fortgeschrittene linguistische Analysen ein.
description.ru: Погрузитесь в классический арабский язык, риторику и продвинутый лингвистический анализ.
description.zh: 深入研究古典阿拉伯语、修辞学和高级语言分析。

lessons.content: Most Arabic nouns and verbs change their endings (Mu'rab), but some are fixed (Mabni) regardless of their position. Mabni include pronouns, demonstratives, and past tense verbs.

examples.english: Qatala (He killed - Past tense is Mabni on Fatha)

lessons.content: Nouns are Marfu' when they are: Subject (Fa'il), Nominal Subject (Mubtada), Predicate (Khabar), Name of Kana, Predicate of Inna.
Nouns are Mansub when they are: Object (Maf'ul), Adverb (Zarf), State (Hal), Specification (Tamyiz), Name of Inna, Predicate of Kana.

examples.english: Ra'aytu al-hilala (I saw the crescent - Maf'ul Bihi)

lessons.content: Nouns are Majrur when preceded by a preposition or are the second part of Idafa.

examples.english: Kitabu at-talibi (The student's book - Mudaf Ilayh)

lessons.content: The Present Tense verb is Majzum when preceded by a Jazim particle (e.g., Lam, La An-Nahiya).

examples.english: Lam yalid wa-lam yulad (He neither begot nor was He begotten)

lessons.content: Most Arabic words are derived from a 3-letter root (Jidhr). The pattern (Wazn) determines the meaning (e.g., doer, place, tool).
Deriving verbs from nouns or other verbs to add meanings like intensity, reciprocity, or causation.

examples.english: Hajja (performed Hajj) -> Tahajja (argued with each other)

lessons.content: Forming Active Participle (Ism Fa'il), Passive Participle (Ism Maf'ul), and Verbal Noun (Masdar).
Rules of 'Ilal (Vowel alteration) and Ibdal (Substitution) in weak verbs (Mithal, Ajwaf, Naqis).

examples.english: Qala (He said) - Originally 'Qawala' (Waw changed to Alif)

lessons.content: Study of sentence meanings and structures (Context). Includes: Khabar (Informative) vs Insha (Performative), Ijaz (Conciseness), Itnab (Elaboration).

examples.english: Rabbi inni wahana al-'azmu minni (My Lord, indeed my bones have weakened - Showing weakness/prayer)

lessons.content: Study of imagery and figures of speech. Includes: Tashbih (Simile), Isti'ara (Metaphor), Kinaya (Metonymy).

examples.english: Zaidun ka-al-asadi (Zaid is like a lion - Simile)
Ra'aytu asadan yakhtubu (I saw a lion giving a speech - Metaphor for a brave man)

lessons.content: Study of rhetorical embellishments. Includes: Saj' (Rhymed Prose), Jinas (Pun/Paronomasia), Tibaq (Antithesis).

examples.english: Wa-tahsabuhum ayqazan wa-hum ruqud (You would think them awake, while they were asleep - Tibaq)

lessons.content: Detailed grammatical analysis of Quranic verses, highlighting unique structures and eloquence.
Analyzing classical poetry (Mu'allaqat) for grammatical structure and rhetorical devices.

examples.arabic: قِفَا نَبْكِ مِنْ ذِكْرَى حَبِيبٍ وَمَنْزِلِ
examples.english: Qifa (Imperative Dual) nabki (Jussive Answer to Command) - Stop, let us weep...

lessons.content: A comparison where one party is omitted. 'In the library are stars' (meaning scholars).

examples.english: Wa-ishta'ala ar-ra'su shayban (And the head flared up with gray hair - Metaphor for aging spreading like fire)

lessons.content: comparing two things using particles like 'Ka' or 'Mithla'. 'Anta ka-al-qamari' (You are like the moon).

examples.english: Al-'ilmu ka-an-nuri yahdi (Knowledge is like light, it guides)

lessons.content: Using a phrase to mean something else indirectly. 'Kathir ar-ramad' (Much ash) means generous (cooking for many).

examples.english: Fulanun nazifu al-yadi (So-and-so has a clean hand - Metonymy for honesty)

lessons.content: Sentences that have a grammatical place (e.g., Khabar, Hal, Sifah) and those that don't (e.g., Ibtida'iyyah, Silah).

examples.english: Ra'aytu rajulan yadhaku ('Yadhaku' is a verbal sentence in place of Adjective/Na't for 'Rajulan')

lessons.content: Practical practice on parsing complete paragraphs or verses, focusing on the relationship between sentences and the overall grammatical structure.

examples.english: Idha (Conditional Adverb) ja'a (Past verb) nasru (Fa'il) Allahi (Mudaf Ilayh) wa (Conj) al-fathu (Ma'tuf on Nasru).
